title: Phase diagram of a dual-species Rydberg atom ladder
authors: Lei-Yi-Nan Liu, Shi-Rong Peng, Ze-Yuan Huang, Xing-Man Wei, Yun-Han Zou, Su Yi, Jian Cui
abstract: Dual-species Rydberg atom arrays extend single-species platforms by introducing competing interaction scales and enhanced quantum fluctuations, enabling phenomena beyond homogeneous settings. In this work, we study the ground-state phase diagram of a one-dimensional dual-species Rydberg atom ladder using large-scale density-matrix renormalization group calculations. We identify disordered phases, multiple ordered phases with $\mathbb{Z}_2$, $\mathbb{Z}_3$, and $\mathbb{Z}_4$ symmetry, as well as floating phases characterized by incommensurate wave vectors and algebraically decaying correlations. Importantly, we observe a smooth crossover between distinct $\mathbb{Z}_2$-ordered regimes, reflecting a reorganization of low-energy degrees of freedom rather than a true phase transition, which is absent in single-species Rydberg arrays. We further uncover a multi-critical point at the boundary between the $\mathbb{Z}_2 \otimes \mathbb{Z}_2$ and $\mathbb{Z}_3 \otimes \mathbb{Z}_3$ ordered phases, where Ising, chiral, and first-order transition lines intersect. Our results demonstrate that dual-species Rydberg atom arrays provide a unique platform for realizing crossover physics and multi-critical behavior inaccessible in single-species architectures.
summary: This paper explores the phase diagram of a 1D ladder made of two different species of Rydberg atoms. By using DMRG simulations, the authors find a variety of ordered and floating phases, including Z2, Z3, and Z4 symmetries. A key finding is the discovery of a multi-critical point where different types of phase transitions meet. The study also highlights a smooth crossover between certain phases, a feature unique to the dual-species setup. This work shows that mixed-species Rydberg arrays are a powerful platform for studying advanced many-body physics.
main_problem: The study investigates the ground-state phase diagram and the nature of quantum phase transitions in a one-dimensional dual-species Rydberg atom ladder.
main_result: The authors identify multiple ordered phases (Z2, Z3, Z4), floating phases, and a smooth crossover between Z2 regimes, as well as a multi-critical point where Ising, chiral, and first-order transition lines intersect.
method: Large-scale Density-Matrix Renormal-group (DMRG) calculations using the ITensor library, supplemented by finite-size scaling and Conformal Field Theory (CFT) analysis.
model_or_system: A 1D ladder geometry consisting of two species of Rydberg atoms (Type-A and Type-B, specifically modeled after Cs and Rb) with competing van der Waals interactions and single-site driving.
key_observables: Bipartite entanglement entropy, Binder cumulants, order parameters, correlation functions, structure factor, and the Luttinger parameter.
important_parameters: Rabi frequency (Omega), detuning (Delta), interaction scales (CAA, CBB, CAB), and the ratio of interaction range to lattice spacing (Rb/d).
main_assumptions: Identical Rabi frequencies and detunings for both atomic species; use of an effective interaction cutoff length.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the ladder lattice schematic, energy levels, and Rydberg states; Figure 2 presents the ground-state phase diagram; Figure 6 and 7 illustrate wave vector behavior and the CFT critical point at phase boundaries; Figure 9 shows the structure factor peaks.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the dual-species ladder platform, details the Hamiltonian and numerical methodology, presents the identified phase diagram, analyzes the nature of specific phase transitions and multi-criticality, and concludes with the physical implications for Rydberg arrays.
why_interesting: It demonstrates how dual-species architectures enable complex phenomena like chiral criticality and multi-critical points that are inaccessible in standard single-species Rydberg arrays, providing a rich playground for studying 1D quantum phase transitions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Ladder lattice of two-species Rydberg atoms. Blue (A) and orange (B) circles denote the two atomic species, with lattice spacing d along both legs and rungs. The van der Waals interactions are characterized by CAA 6 , CAB 6 , and CBB 6 . (b) Illustration of energy levels, detunings ∆and external fields Ωof A and B atoms.

FIG. 2. (a) Ground-state phase diagram of the ladder system. Four distinct ordered phases are identified, as illustrated in (c). The phase boundaries are determined from the bipartite entanglement entropy S and are indicated by white dashed lines. (b) Enlarged view of the region highlighted by the white square in (a). This regime contains three ordered phases, one disordered phase, and several floating-phase regions. Blue (red) lines denote first-order (continuous) phase transitions. Floating phases are marked by or enclosed within orange lines. The green dot indicates the multi-critical point, while the red dot marks a CFT point belonging to the Ashkin-Teller universality class. FIG. 4. (a) Binder cumulant U B 2 for different system sizes at c3 cut (y = Rb/d = 0.86). (b) Inverse correlation length 1/ξ extracted from finite systems of size L = 301 and L = 601 by fitting the correlation functions Cs(x). Error bars indicate one standard deviation. The green line shows the result obtained from VUMPS [52] with bond dimension χ = 200.

FIG. 3. Finite size scaling analysis of binder cumulant and order parameter at cut c1 and c2. We extract critical ex- ponents β, ν and the position of critical point xc for c1 cut in (a)(c), and for c2 cut in (b)(d). We fit the data using a 14th-order polynomial by minimizing the distance between the data points and the polynomial curve.

FIG. 6. Illustration of the wave vector q near the boundary between the disordered phase and the Z3(⊗Z3)- and Z2(⊗Z2)- ordered phases. The blue lines denote the phase boundaries extracted from the crossing points of the Binder cumulant for different system sizes. The green line marks the boundary be- tween the commensurate disorder (CD) and incommensurate disorder (ID) phases. In the green (orange) shaded region, the wave vector takes the commensurate value q = 1/2 (q = 1/3). The orange line indicates the commensurate line within the ID region: above this line, the wave vector satisfies q < 1/3, while below it q > 1/3.

FIG. 5. (a) Data collapse of the Binder cumulant along cut c4, yielding a critical exponent ν ≈0.82. The data are fitted using a 20th-order polynomial. (b) The phase transition indicator ξ|q −1/p| along cut c4 approaches a finite, nonzero constant, indicating a chiral phase transition. (c) The Binder cumulant along cut c5 exhibits a sudden drop, signaling the emergence of a floating phase. (d) The corresponding phase- transition indicator along cut c5 diverges, indicating a KT transition.

FIG. 7. Illustration of the wave vector q near the boundary between the disordered phase and the Z4 ⊗Z4 ordered phases. The blue lines indicate the phase boundaries extracted from the crossing points of the Binder cumulant for different system sizes. The orange shaded region corresponds to the commen- surate regime where the wave vector is fixed at q = 1/4. The orange line denotes the commensurate line within the incom- mensurate disordered (ID) region: above this line the wave vector satisfies q < 1/4, while below it q > 1/4. The CFT point, defined as the intersection of the phase boundary and the q = 1/4 line, is highlighted by a large red dot.

Fig. 8 (a). From this analysis, we extract the critical ex- ponent ν and the critical location xc. In Fig. 8 (b), we determine the central charge c ≈1 and the dynamical critical exponent z ≈1, confirming that this transition point is indeed described by a conformal field theory. The correlation lengths obtained from the correlation func- tions are consistent with the previously extracted ν and xc, as shown in Fig. 8 (c). Moreover, as shown in Fig. 8 (d), the indicator ξ|q − 1/4| approaches zero near the critical point along the c6 cut, signaling a conformal phase transition. FIG. 8. (a) Data collapse of the Binder cumulant along c6 cut (CFT cut, y = −0.1442x + 0.9687), yielding a critical exponent ν ≈0.786. The data are fitted using a 20th-order polynomial. (b) Central charge c and dynamical exponent z (inset) are extracted at the critical point xc. (c) The correlation length at the disorder side show good agreement with the extracted exponent and xc from (a). (d-f) Phase transition indicators along different cuts.

(a) Friedel oscillations of the local excitation density ˜nj, fitted using Eq.(20). Blue dots denote the DMRG data, while orange open circles indicate the fitting results. (b) Entanglement entropy S(ρℓ) for bi-partitions ℓ|(L −ℓ), used to extract the central charge c. Here ρℓis the reduced density matrix of the first ℓrungs. Blue dots and the orange solid line represent the DMRG data and the corresponding fit, respectively. Inset: Normalized Fourier transforms of the fitting residuals, denoted by e F[e].
